title: Parametric velocity and speed
description: Solve for the velocity or speed of a parametric curve algebraically or at a certain time t.

hints:
The velocity of a parametric curve is the derivative of its position. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t))$, then the velocity is:

$f'(t) = (a'(t), b'(t))$

Our position function here is $f(t)$.

$f'(t) = (22, 99\cos(3t))$
Therefore, the velocity of $f(t)$ is $(22, 99\cos(3t))$.

The velocity of a parametric curve is the derivative of its position. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t))$, then the velocity is:

$f'(t) = (a'(t), b'(t))$

Our position function here is $h(t)$.

$h'(t) = (8t, 10t \sin(t^2))$
Therefore, the velocity of $h(t)$ is $(8t, 10t \sin(t^2))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$g(t) = (e^{2t}, t\sin(t))$

**What is the velocity of $g(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The velocity of a parametric curve is the derivative of its position. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t))$, then the velocity is:

$f'(t) = (a'(t), b'(t))$

Our position function here is $g(t)$.

$g'(t) = (2e^{2t}, \sin(t) + t\cos(t))$
Therefore, the velocity of $g(t)$ is $(2e^{2t}, \sin(t) + t\cos(t))$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$v(t) = (10\cos(t), 10\sin(t), 100 - t)$

**What is the velocity of $v(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The velocity of a parametric curve is the derivative of its position. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t), c(t))$, then the velocity is:

$f'(t) = (a'(t), b'(t), c'(t))$

Our position function here is $v(t)$.

$v'(t) = (-10\sin(t), 10\cos(t), -1)$
Therefore, the velocity of $v(t)$ is $(-10\sin(t), 10\cos(t), -1)$.

The velocity of a parametric curve is the derivative of its position. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t), c(t))$, then the velocity is:

$f'(t) = (a'(t), b'(t), c'(t))$

Our position function here is $v(t)$.

$\begin{align}
v'(t) &= (-\sin^2(t) + \cos^2(t), 2\sin(t)\cos(t), -\sin(t)) \\ \\
&= (1 - 2\sin^2(t), \sin(2t), -\sin(t))
\end{align}$

Therefore, the velocity of $v(t)$ is:

 $(1 - 2\sin^2(t), \sin(2t), -\sin(t))$

assessment_items.question_content:
$p(t) = (4t - 3t^2, t + 5, t^8 - 4t^2)$

**What is the velocity of $p(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The velocity of a parametric curve is the derivative of its position. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t), c(t))$, then the velocity is:

$f'(t) = (a'(t), b'(t), c'(t))$

Our position function here is $p(t)$.

$p'(t) = (4 - 6t, 1, 8t^7 - 8t)$
Therefore, the velocity of $p(t)$ is $(4 - 6t, 1, 8t^7 - 8t)$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$s(t) = (t^4, t^4)$

**What is the speed of $s(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The speed of a parametric curve is the magnitude of its velocity. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t))$, then speed is:

$\| f'(t) \| = \sqrt{ a'(t)^2 + b'(t)^2 }$

Our position function here is $s(t)$.

$\begin{align}
s'(t) &= (4t^3, 4t^3) \\ \\
\text{speed} &= ||s'(t)|| \\ \\
&= \sqrt{16t^6 + 16t^6} \\ \\
&= 4t^3\sqrt{2}
\end{align}$
Therefore, the speed of $s(t)$ is $4t^3\sqrt{2}$.

The speed of a parametric curve is the magnitude of its velocity. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t))$, then speed is:

$\| f'(t) \| = \sqrt{ a'(t)^2 + b'(t)^2 }$

Our position function here is $r(t)$.

$\begin{align}
r'(t) &= (9t^2 - 2, 10t) \\ \\
\text{speed} &= ||r'(t)|| \\ \\
&= \sqrt{81t^4 - 36t^2 + 4 + 100t^2} \\ \\
&= \sqrt{81t^4 + 64t^2 + 4}
\end{align}$
Therefore, the speed of $r(t)$ is $\sqrt{81t^4 + 64t^2 + 4}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$r(t) = (t\sin(t), t^2)$

**What is the speed of $r(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The speed of a parametric curve is the magnitude of its velocity. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t))$, then speed is:

$\| f'(t) \| = \sqrt{ a'(t)^2 + b'(t)^2 }$

Our position function here is $r(t)$.

$\begin{align}
r'(t) &= (\sin(t) + t\cos(t), 2t) \\ \\
\text{speed} &= ||r'(t)|| \\ \\
&= \sqrt{\sin^2(t) + 2t\sin(t)\cos(t) + t^2\cos^2(t) + 4t^2} \\ \\
&= \sqrt{\sin^2(t) + t\sin(2t) +t^2\cos^2(t) + 4t^2}
\end{align}$

Therefore, the speed of $r(t)$ is

$\sqrt{\sin^2(t) + t\sin(2t) +t^2\cos^2(t) + 4t^2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
$p(t) = (t, e^t, t)$

**What is the speed of $p(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The speed of a parametric curve is the magnitude of its velocity. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t), c(t))$, then speed is:

$\| f'(t) \| = \sqrt{ a'(t)^2 + b'(t)^2 + c'(t)^2 }$

Our position function here is $p(t)$.

$\begin{align}
p'(t) &= (1, e^t, 1) \\ \\
\text{speed} &= ||p'(t)|| \\ \\
&= \sqrt{1 + e^{2t} + 1} \\ \\
&= \sqrt{2 + e^{2t}}
\end{align}$
Therefore, the speed of $p(t)$ is $\sqrt{2 + e^{2t}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$p(t) = (3\sin(2t), 3\cos(2t), t^2)$

**What is the speed of $p(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The speed of a parametric curve is the magnitude of its velocity. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t), c(t))$, then speed is:

$\| f'(t) \| = \sqrt{ a'(t)^2 + b'(t)^2 + c'(t)^2 }$

Our position function here is $p(t)$.

$\begin{align}
p'(t) &= (6\cos(2t), -6\sin(2t), 2t) \\ \\
\text{speed} &= ||p'(t)|| \\ \\
&= \sqrt{36\cos^2(2t) + 36\sin^2(2t) + 4t^2} \\ \\
&= \sqrt{36 + 4t^2} \\ \\
&= 2\sqrt{9 + t^2}
\end{align}$
Therefore, the speed of $p(t)$ is $2\sqrt{9 + t^2}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$v(t) = (t^4, 2t^3, t^4)$

**What is the speed of $v(t)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The speed of a parametric curve is the magnitude of its velocity. If $f(t) = (a(t), b(t), c(t))$, then speed is:

$\| f'(t) \| = \sqrt{ a'(t)^2 + b'(t)^2 + c'(t)^2 }$

Our position function here is $v(t)$.

$\begin{align}
v'(t) &= (4t^3, 6t^2, 4t^3) \\ \\
\text{speed} &= ||v'(t)|| \\ \\
&= \sqrt{16t^6 + 36t^4 + 16t^6} \\ \\
&= \sqrt{32t^6 + 36t^4} \\ \\
&= 2t^2\sqrt{8t^2 + 9}
\end{align}$
Therefore, the speed of $v(t)$ is $2t^2\sqrt{8t^2 + 9}$.
